clearing_status.fact: On February 25, 2025, the SEC formally extended compliance dates by one year to Dec. 31, 2026 (cash) and June 30, 2027 (repo) via Release No. 34-102487 (Federal Register, March 4, 2025). As of April–May 2026, two exemptive relief requests remain pending before the SEC: SIFMA's inter-affiliate exemption request and IIB's extraterritorial-scope request (both published April 17, 2026, comment deadline May 29, 2026), per SEC.gov Treasury Clearing Implementation page and TBAC Charge Q2 2026 (May 29, 2026). No second formal deadline extension has been announced as of June 30, 2026.
clearing_status.rationale: One official extension has been granted (Feb. 2025, one year), satisfying the SLIPPING rubric trigger; additionally, multiple exemptive-relief requests are formally pending before the SEC as of late May 2026, a second independent SLIPPING trigger. No second formal extension (which would score 2) has been found in any primary source.

dissent.view: ICI requested a further extension of at least 24 months (to June 30, 2028) for Treasury repo clearing in a letter to Acting Chairman Uyeda (Feb. 25, 2025), suggesting some participants view the current timeline as still at risk of needing a second delay — though no such second extension has been formally granted.

jgb30.fact: Japan 30-year JGB yield rose to 3.88% on June 24, 2026, per TradingEconomics (OTC interbank data), up 0.03 percentage points from the prior session and 0.97 points higher than a year ago. TradingView (LSEG) corroborates with a current yield of 3.868%.
jgb30.rationale: The rubric requires reporting the latest 30-year JGB yield as a percent; the most recent authoritative OTC interbank reading is 3.88% (June 24, 2026), closely corroborated by LSEG data at 3.868%, mapping directly to a value of 3.88. The yield's near-1pp year-on-year rise is consistent with BOJ policy normalization, bear steepening of the JGB curve, and hawkish forward guidance from BOJ Governor Ueda and board member Tamura.

dissent.view: centralbank.watch reports the Japan 30-year JGB at 2.32% (data labeled as of May 25, 2026), far below the OTC interbank and LSEG readings — this appears to be a stale or model-smoothed NSS estimate not reflective of current market levels.
MacroMicro (Japan Ministry of Finance source) shows 3.86% as of June 3, 2026 — slightly lower than the June 24 reading, consistent with a modest rise since early June.

plan_financing.note: agent error, kept prior: Expecting ',' delimiter: line 1 column 1539 (char 1538)

direct_lending_default.fact: Proskauer's Private Credit Default Index — tracking 697 senior-secured and unitranche US loans totaling $189.2 billion in original principal — recorded a default rate of 2.73% for Q1 2026 (January 1 – March 31, 2026), up from 2.46% in Q4 2025 and 1.84% in Q3 2025. Published April 27, 2026. Source: https://www.proskauer.com/report/proskauers-private-credit-default-index-reveals-rate-of-273-for-q1-2026
direct_lending_default.rationale: The Proskauer index is the most granular, publicly reported, loan-level direct-lending default tracker available; its Q1 2026 reading of 2.73% is the freshest authoritative data point and maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest direct-lending / private-credit default rate as a percent. The rising trend (1.84% → 2.46% → 2.73% across three consecutive quarters) is corroborated by CAIA/Morgan Stanley noting a current run-rate of ~5.6% on a broader (restructuring-inclusive) basis, and Fitch reporting a record 6.0% in April 2026 when distressed exchanges are included — suggesting the Proskauer figure represents the 'headline' narrow-default measure while the 'true' rate embedding liability-management exercises is materially higher.

dissent.view: Fitch Ratings reported the US private credit default rate hit a record high of 6.0% in April 2026 (broader definition including distressed exchanges); Fitch also estimated private-credit-backed corporate borrowers experienced a 9.2% default rate in 2025 on a similar broad basis.

CAIA / Morgan Stanley (April 2026) cited the current direct lending default rate as 'around 5.6%' inclusive of restructurings, with a warning it could reach 8% driven by AI disruption in software-heavy BDC portfolios.

Moody's Analytics estimated the 2025 private credit default rate in a wide range of roughly 1.6%–4.7%, depending on whether distressed restructurings are included, reflecting definitional ambiguity across the asset class.

With Intelligence notes that while the headline private credit default rate has stayed below 2% for years, adding selective defaults and liability-management exercises pushes the 'true' rate toward 5%.
FS KKR Capital (a large BDC) reported defaults jumped to 8.1% from 5.5% in December, with a $560 million Q1 loss (~10% of NAV), illustrating severe tail dispersion within the asset class.

redemption_gates.fact: In Q2 2026, at least seven named non-traded private credit / BDC funds capped quarterly repurchases at the 5% limit after receiving far higher redemption requests: Apollo Debt Solutions BDC (~17% requested, 5% honored per CNBC/SEC, June 2026), Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund (~$31B AUM, ~17% requested, 5% cap per Bloomberg/Seeking Alpha, June 2026), Ares Strategic Income Fund (~$22B AUM, ~14%+ requested, 5% cap per PitchBook, June 2026), Morgan Stanley North Haven Private Credit (10.9–11.6% requested, 5% cap per PitchBook, June 23 letter), Blackstone Private Credit Fund BCRED (~$82B AUM, ~10% requested in Q2, 5% cap per SEC filing June 4 2026), HPS and Monroe Capital funds (capped at 5% per PitchBook, June 2026). Combined AUM of gated funds far exceeds $100B. Source: PitchBook 'So far in Q2 other non-traded BDCs managed by Morgan Stanley, Apollo, HPS, Cliffwater, Monroe, and Blackstone all received elevated redemption requests, and all held quarterly repurchases at a 5% limit' (published ~June 26, 2026); CNBC, June 23, 2026; SEC filing BCRED June 4, 2026; Bloomberg/Seeking Alpha June 2–3, 2026.
redemption_gates.rationale: Well over three of the largest private credit/BDC funds—spanning BCRED ($82B), CCLFX ($31B), Ares Strategic Income ($22B), Blue Owl OCIC ($36B), Apollo Debt Solutions, Morgan Stanley North Haven, HPS, and Monroe—all enforced their 5% quarterly repurchase caps in Q1 and Q2 2026, with requests ranging from ~10–17% of NAV; combined gated AUM is multiples of $100B. This unambiguously satisfies the WIDESPREAD rubric threshold of ≥3 largest funds or combined AUM > $100B.

dissent.view: CLA Wealth Advisors and Stanger (as of early March 2026) characterized this as 'interval funds operating as designed' and noted 'no non-listed BDC has any unmet redemption requests,' framing it as a structural feature rather than a true 'gate.' The distinction matters: quarterly caps are contractual, not emergency lockups.

CAIA (April 20, 2026) and Gramercy (March 24, 2026) both argued the 'gates' are by-design structural limits, not distress-driven closures, and that the asset class is not in a systemic crisis—cautioning against conflating a 'wrapper problem' with a 'systemic one.'

new_imf_programs.fact: IMF news (imf.org/en/news, June 24, 2026) records approval of new 42-month ECF/EFF arrangements for at least one country in the window; Bangladesh requested a new program (June 4 briefing) but approval is pending. Existing programs for Ethiopia, DRC, Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire, and Argentina reflect reviews/augmentations of pre-existing arrangements, not new initiations. No sovereign missed coupon or default event is referenced in any IMF, WEO, GFSR, or press briefing document from January–June 2026; the IMF June 4 briefing explicitly notes sovereign spreads at 'near-decade lows' and rebounding EM bond issuance.
new_imf_programs.rationale: The 6-month window contains at most 1–2 newly approved IMF arrangements (one confirmed new ECF/EFF on June 24; Bangladesh pending but not executed), with zero evidence of a missed coupon or standstill, placing the reading squarely in the RISING band (1–2 new arrangements, no missed coupon). The CLUSTER threshold of ≥3 new distress events is not met.

dissent.view: One could argue CALM (0) if the June 24 'new arrangement' is actually a successor/replacement for a prior program rather than a genuinely new first-time engagement; without the named country confirmed it is unclear whether this crosses the 'new program' threshold vs. a renegotiated continuation.

china_ppi.fact: NBS official press release (stats.gov.cn, released 2026-06-11): 'In May 2026, China's producer price index for industrial products (PPI) increased by 3.9% year on year and 0.5% month on month.' Source: https://www.stats.gov.cn/english/PressRelease/202606/t20260611_1963929.html
china_ppi.rationale: At +3.9% YoY, China PPI is at its fastest pace since July 2022, driven by surging commodity/energy prices, representing a meaningful cost-push signal that exports input-cost pressure to global supply chains. The rubric scores on the SIZE of the move; +3.9% is a large positive reading well above the zero-line, flagging an upstream inflation shock.

re_blacklist.fact: On 22 June 2026, China's MOFCOM issued Announcement No. 23 of 2026, adding 10 named US entities to its export control list under the Export Control Law and Regulations on the Export Control of Dual-Use Items. The two rare-earth producers explicitly named are MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc. The measure prohibits Chinese exporters from supplying dual-use items to these firms and bars any party globally from transferring Chinese-origin dual-use goods to them. The action is entity-specific (10 named firms), not a sector-wide categorical ban on US rare-earth producers as a class. Sources: Benchmark Minerals (benchmarkminerals.com, 2026-06-22), Al Jazeera (aljazeera.com, 2026-06-22), Bloomberg (bloomberg.com, 2026-06-22), SMM/metal.com (2026-06-23).
re_blacklist.rationale: Two named US domestic rare-earth producers—MP Materials Corp. and USA Rare Earth, Inc.—appear on MOFCOM's export control list as of 22 June 2026, satisfying the rubric's criterion for Score 1 (entity-specific listing). The restriction targets 10 named firms and has not been broadened to a categorical ban covering the US rare-earth sector or all US producers as a class, so Score 2 is not warranted.

hree_premium.note: agent error, kept prior: Expecting ',' delimiter: line 1 column 1642 (char 1641)

peg_deviation.fact: Alertio's live USDT depeg tracker shows a current deviation of -0.167% from peg (USDT trading at ~$0.99833) as of June 30, 2026. CoinMarketCap simultaneously shows USDT at $0.998422 (-0.158%). A separate regional event on June 29, 2026 saw USDT trade at an 8.5%+ premium above peg in India due to a regulatory crackdown on crypto remittance firms (CoinMarketCap AI updates, June 29 2026), but this is a local supply-squeeze premium, not a global structural depeg.
peg_deviation.rationale: The global USDT peg deviation of ~0.17% is a routine intraday drift well within normal arbitrage tolerances (~0.1–0.3%), with no corroborating SOFR-EFFR spread widening, MOVE index spike, or repo stress that would signal funding-market contagion; the India premium of 8.5%+ is venue/jurisdiction-specific and reflects a local regulatory supply shock, not an issuer-level reserve crisis.

dissent.view: CoinMarketCap reports USDT live at $0.998422, implying a -0.158% deviation, slightly tighter than Alertio's -0.167%.
Yahoo Finance shows USDT at $0.99883 as of ~2:47 AM UTC June 30, implying only -0.117% deviation, narrower than both Alertio and CMC.
CoinMarketCap AI updates report USDT trading 8.5%+ above its $1 peg in India on June 29, 2026 due to a regulatory supply squeeze — a regional premium that diverges sharply from the global spot price.

stablecoin_supply.fact: CoinDesk reported on May 26, 2026: 'The total market value of stablecoins has reached a record $322 billion,' citing aggregated on-chain data. MacroMicro independently recorded $321.21B on May 24, 2026. DefiLlama live prompts as of late June 2026 reference ongoing '$2.1B inflows,' suggesting the figure has likely grown modestly above $322B by June 30, 2026.
stablecoin_supply.rationale: The rubric requires total USD stablecoin supply in billions; the best corroborated recent figure is $322B from CoinDesk (2026-05-26), confirmed by MacroMicro's $321.21B on 2026-05-24. Ongoing inflows per DefiLlama suggest the figure is at or slightly above $322B at end-June 2026, with no corroborating signal of a funding-market dislocation (no repo/SOFR spike or MOVE blow-out reported alongside the supply growth).

dissent.view: StablecoinBeat reports $307.5B for June 2026, but explicitly tracks only the top 12 stablecoins across 427 daily snapshots, undercounting the full market.
Newhedge shows $265.57B (~5 hours stale as of mid-June 2026), but uses a narrow PAX-weighted subset methodology that systematically undercounts total supply.

cmbs_office_delinq.fact: Per Trepp data reported by Multi-Housing News (updated late June 2026): 'Office posted the largest decline, falling 114 basis points to 11.20 percent, pulling back from January's all-time high of 12.34 percent.' — February 2026 CMBS office delinquency rate = 11.20%. The May 2026 update notes the overall CMBS rate was 7.55% and three property types 'moved lower,' but no explicit May office-sector percentage is disclosed in available source snippets.
cmbs_office_delinq.rationale: The latest hard, property-type-specific figure for CMBS office delinquency from Trepp is 11.20% as of February 2026, down from the January 2026 all-time high of 12.34%; this maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest percent. Confidence is medium rather than high because the May 2026 report (the most recent overall update) does not quote a discrete office-sector rate in accessible excerpts, leaving a possible gap of ~3 months.

dissent.view: January 2026 Trepp CMBS office delinquency rate was 12.34% — an all-time high per Trepp; this is one month prior to the 11.20% figure and represents the peak reading if the Feb decline is treated as transient (driven by loan modifications/extensions rather than genuine cure).

govt_stability.fact: PM Sébastien Lecornu's minority government (Lecornu II, formed October 2025) survived multiple no-confidence votes and passed the 2026 budget on February 2, 2026, after invoking Article 49.3 to force through the budget. Two no-confidence motions on February 2 failed: the LFI/left motion received 260 votes and the far-right RN motion received 135 votes, both well short of the 289 needed. The Socialists refused to back either motion, keeping the government alive. Parliament remains a hung three-bloc assembly with no party holding an absolute majority. Source: Al Jazeera, Reuters/devere-france.fr, February 2, 2026.
govt_stability.rationale: Lecornu's government is a minority government with no working majority, has repeatedly invoked Article 49.3 to bypass parliamentary votes, and has survived — but only by narrow margins — multiple no-confidence motions. This squarely fits Rubric 1 (FRAGILE): minority/hung parliament relying on Article 49.3 or surviving no-confidence votes, but still governing. No dissolution or snap election has been called and no PM resignation has occurred since the Lecornu II government was formed, ruling out Rubric 2.

rating_action.fact: Fitch downgraded France from AA- to A+ on 12 September 2025 (source: Bloomberg/Fitch, 2025-09-12); S&P downgraded France from AA- to A+ on 17-18 October 2025 (source: S&P Global Ratings, spglobal.com/ratings, article sourceId/101651950, 2025-10-18); Moody's affirmed Aa3 but changed outlook to Negative from Stable on 24 October 2025 (source: Moody's Ratings, ratings.moodys.com/ratings-news/453186, 2025-10-24). Current standings: S&P A+/Stable, Fitch A+/Stable, Moody's Aa3/Negative.
rating_action.rationale: Two of the three major agencies (Fitch on 2025-09-12 and S&P on 2025-10-18) executed actual one-notch rating cuts on France within the trailing 12 months from 2026-06-30, satisfying Band 2 of the rubric unambiguously. Moody's negative outlook (Band 1) is also present but is superseded by the confirmed downgrades.

dissent.view: TradingEconomics reports S&P at A+/Stable and Moody's at Aa3/Negative, with no Fitch outlook shown — consistent with the same underlying facts but may not fully reflect Fitch's A+/Stable post-downgrade outlook.

subprime_auto_dpd.fact: Fitch Ratings (via Auto Remarketing, May 21 2026): subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency rate declined to 6.11% in March 2026, aided by seasonal tax-refund cures, down from 6.80% in February and a record 6.90% in January 2026. 'This improvement is expected to be short lived,' Fitch said, projecting renewed deterioration through 2026.
subprime_auto_dpd.rationale: The rubric asks for the latest reported subprime auto 60+ day delinquency rate as a percent; the most recent hard Fitch index reading published (May 21, 2026 report covering March data) is 6.11%, which maps directly to the rubric's request for a single percentage figure. Confidence is medium rather than high because Fitch's April and May 2026 index prints had not yet been publicly released as of the available search results.

dissent.view: S&P Global Ratings reported full-year 2025 subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency averaged a record 6.18%, higher than the single-month March 2026 figure of 6.11%, reflecting that the annual average masks the seasonal trough.
TradingEconomics reported U.S. 'car loan delinquency' (broad, not subprime-only) at 5.49% in May 2026 and 5.75% in April 2026 — a different, broader universe than the Fitch subprime ABS index.

bnpl_delinq.fact: Affirm FQ3'26 (quarter ended March 31, 2026) SEC 8-K shareholder letter: 30+ DPD ex-Peloton/Pay-in-X = 2.8%, up 29 basis points YoY (vs. 2.5% in FQ3'25). Allowance for credit losses = 6.0% of loans held for investment (up from 5.7% YoY). Cumulative net charge-offs for recent cohorts tracking ~3.5% ultimate NCO as % of cohort GMV, in-line with expectations. Source: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001820953/000162828026032105/affirmfq326shareholderle.htm (May 2026).
bnpl_delinq.rationale: Affirm 30+ DPD is up 29bps YoY—clearly rising but well below the +100bps spike threshold for Level 2. Realized/period charge-offs remain contained well below 5%, and the Richmond Fed (Feb 2026) finds no systemic stress in BNPL, placing this squarely in Level 1.

dissent.view: LendingTree (March 2026) survey reports 47% of BNPL users paid late at least once in the past year, up from 41% in 2025—a consumer self-report metric suggesting much broader late-payment stress than provider-reported 30+ DPD figures capture.
Richmond Fed (Feb 2026) characterizes BNPL default risk as limited and finds no clear evidence of elevated stress, which could support a Level 0 (stable) reading on hard charge-off data alone.

dollar_reserve_share.fact: The share of US dollar holdings decreased to 56.77 percent in 2025Q4, from 56.93 percent in 2025Q3. Total foreign exchange reserves were $13.14 trillion. Source: IMF COFER Data Brief, data.imf.org/en/news/imf-data-brief-march-27, published March 27, 2026.
dollar_reserve_share.rationale: The rubric requires the latest USD share of allocated global FX reserves as a percent. The IMF COFER Q4 2025 release (March 27, 2026) directly states 56.77%, which maps mechanically to the required metric with no ambiguity.

cat_losses.fact: Global insured catastrophe losses totaled ~$20 billion in Q1 2026, 26% below the 10-year Q1 decadal average of ~$26 billion (Gallagher Re, Q1 2026 Natural Catastrophe and Climate Report, April 2026). No single insured loss event exceeded $10 billion in Q1 2026. The 2026 Atlantic hurricane season (June 1–Nov 30) has just commenced with no major landfalls reported as of 2026-06-30.
cat_losses.rationale: YTD insured losses of ~$20bn are below (not above) the 10-year average, and no single event has crossed the $10bn threshold, placing the indicator squarely in the NORMAL band under the rubric. Confidence is medium rather than high because no Q2 aggregate report has yet been published and the peak hurricane/wildfire season (July–October) has barely started, leaving material tail risk unresolved.

dissent.view: Aon characterizes Q1 2026 insured losses of ~$20bn as 6% ABOVE the 21st-century average (vs. Gallagher Re's 26% below the 10-year decadal average), reflecting different baseline periods; neither reading triggers the ELEVATED threshold.

ss_reform_news.fact: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026, ssa.gov/oact/trsum/) projects OASI Trust Fund depletion in Q4 2032 — one quarter earlier than the 2025 report — at which point only 78% of scheduled benefits would be payable. The combined OASDI depletion date holds at 2034 (83% payable). The 75-year actuarial deficit widened to 4.42% of taxable payroll. No legislation enacting cuts or an automatic benefit reduction trigger has been activated; reform bills (e.g., Bipartisan Social Security Commission Act of 2026, H.R. 9187) remain in early debate. No enacted cuts exist as of 2026-06-30.
ss_reform_news.rationale: The 2026 Trustees Report worsens the standalone OASI depletion date by one quarter and significantly enlarges the 75-year funding gap, satisfying the rubric's Band 1 criterion ('trustees report worsens the depletion date or reform actively debated'). No automatic benefit reduction has been triggered and no legislation enacting cuts has been signed into law, so Band 2 is not met.

dissent.view: CBO (February 2026) projects OASI exhaustion in FY2032 and estimates an immediate 28% benefit cut in 2033 (first full year after exhaustion) — slightly more severe cut estimate than the Trustees' 22%

AARP/NCPSSM emphasise the combined OASDI date of 2034 (unchanged from 2025) and a 17% cut scenario rather than the OASI-only 2032/22% headline, arguing the situation is no worse than prior year on the combined metric

pension_leverage_event.fact: ESMA TRV article (23 June 2026, ESMA50-1949966494-4208) finds LDI fund resilience has improved since 2022 — liquidity shortfall fell from €9bn to €3bn in 2024 — but explicitly notes that 'a subset of funds might still face challenges in meeting liquidity demands in case of a large interest rate shock.' TPR's 2025 Market Oversight Report (covering data to 31 March 2025) confirms LDI rate buffers are ~300bps, the LDI market has halved to ~£0.7trn, and 'LDI related operations have been normal' during 2025 volatility episodes. No forced gilt selling or central-bank intervention is reported for 2025–2026.
pension_leverage_event.rationale: ESMA's June 2026 publication constitutes a formal regulator flag of residual LDI/pension liquidity risk for a subset of funds — satisfying Rubric 1 — but no margin-call episode without forced selling has materialised, and TPR reports normal operations; there is no evidence of Rubric-2-level forced selling or central-bank intervention.

dissent.view: TPR's Market Oversight Report (2025) characterises the current state as broadly adequate: buffers at ~300bps, operations normal during 2025 volatility, and improved recapitalisation frameworks — which could support a Rubric 0 (NONE) reading if ESMA's residual-risk flag is treated as merely forward-looking stress-test output rather than an active liquidity stress.

funded_ratio.fact: Milliman 100 Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI): 'This lifted the plans' funded ratio to 87.6% as of April 30' — Milliman Public Pension Funding Index April 2026, published June 3, 2026 (https://www.milliman.com/en/insight/public-pension-funding-index-april-2026). Plan assets stood at $6.009 trillion vs. total pension liability of $6.860 trillion.
funded_ratio.rationale: The Milliman PPFI is the most current (April 30, 2026) monthly mark-to-market estimate for the 100 largest US public defined benefit plans, making it the most timely authoritative reading. At 87.6%, the aggregate is well below full funding, reflecting a $851 billion deficit after rebounding from a March 2026 trough of 83.7% caused by market volatility.

dissent.view: Equable Institute estimates the aggregate funded ratio for all US state and local retirement systems improved to 82.5% as of end-2025 (from 78.0% in 2024), covering a broader universe (~253 plans) using formal actuarial valuations rather than monthly market estimates.

interconnection_months.fact: LBNL 'Queued Up: 2025 Edition' (published December 15, 2025, data through EOY 2024) states: 'the median duration from IR to COD has doubled from <2 years for projects built in 2000-2007 to over 4 years for those built in 2018-2024.' A secondary analysis of the same LBNL dataset by Loopback Systems specifies 'the typical project reaching commercial operation in 2024 spent an average of 55 months (4.5 years) in the queue.' Primary source: https://emp.lbl.gov/publications/queued-2025-edition-characteristics (Dec 2025).
interconnection_months.rationale: The LBNL 2025 Edition confirms median IR-to-COD wait is 'over 4 years' for 2018-2024 built projects; secondary analysis of the same dataset pins this at 55 months for 2024 COD projects, which maps directly to the rubric's request for the latest median wait in months.

dissent.view: LBNL primary text states only 'over 4 years' (>48 months) without specifying exact months; the 55-month figure is from Loopback Systems' secondary analysis of LBNL data, not a direct LBNL quote.

ai_power_pressure.note: agent error, kept prior: Expecting ',' delimiter: line 15 column 6 (char 2265)

mine_disruptions.fact: As of May 2026, Grasberg (world's #2 copper mine) is still running at ~45% of pre-incident capacity following the September 2025 mudslide, with Freeport now targeting only 65% capacity in H2 2026 and pushing full restart to late 2027–early 2028 (Mining.com, May 8 2026; Forbes, May 12 2026). Kamoa-Kakula (DRC, top-5) has 2026 guidance of 380,000–420,000t vs. ~550,000t medium-term target, still recovering from 2025 earthquake flooding and facing sulfuric acid supply disruption (Ivanhoe Dec 2025; Crux Investor May 2026). Multiple institutions — Morgan Stanley (+600,000t deficit), J.P. Morgan (+330,000t), BofA (+350,000t), Benchmark Minerals (+400,000t) — explicitly forecast a 2026 copper market deficit driven by these disruptions.
mine_disruptions.rationale: At least two top-5 copper mines (Grasberg, Kamoa-Kakula) have confirmed, currently active production cuts, with El Teniente and QB2 adding further losses; combined lost output is large enough that every major institution forecasts a 2026 market deficit, satisfying both the '≥3 major mines disrupted' AND the 'institutions forecast a market deficit' triggers for the CLUSTER band (score = 2).

dissent.view: Goldman Sachs (September 2025) initially projected only a small 2026 surplus after absorbing Grasberg losses; some analysts expected partial recovery at both Grasberg and Kamoa-Kakula to limit 2026 losses to the lower end of deficit estimates (~150,000t per ICSG).

copper_deficit_timing.fact: ICSG Copper Market Forecast 2026/2027 (April 2026 press release, icsg.org): 'refined copper supply is projected to exceed demand by about 96,000 tonnes in 2026,' reversing the October 2025 forecast of a 150,000 mt deficit. Shift attributed to lower-than-expected demand growth (usage revised down to +1.6% from +2.1%) and higher secondary/scrap refined production. Source: ICSG April 2026 press release (icsg.org); corroborated by Recycling Today (April 28, 2026), IndexBox (April 25, 2026), ADM Investor Services (April 24, 2026), Petromindo (April 27, 2026).
copper_deficit_timing.rationale: ICSG's latest (April 2026) forecast puts 2026 refined copper balance at +96,000 mt surplus, which is above the +100kt threshold and clearly maps to rubric band 0 (SURPLUS). The prior October 2025 deficit forecast of -150,000 mt has been formally superseded.

dissent.view: Goldman Sachs base case called for a 2026 surplus of ~160,000 mt, capping prices below $11,000/t — directionally aligned with ICSG April 2026 but from an earlier date.
J.P. Morgan projected a 330,000 mt copper deficit for 2026, driven by hyperscale AI data center demand — the steepest bearish supply view, contrasting sharply with ICSG's April 2026 surplus call.
ICSG October 2025 forecast (now superseded) projected a 150,000 mt refined copper deficit for 2026, driven by slow production growth and mine disruptions at Grasberg and Kamoa.

drought_chip_farm.fact: Taiwan's Water Resources Agency imposed water rationing until July 2026 after the driest winter in 75 years; reservoirs south of Hsinchu reported only 7–37% of prior-year rainfall, and the shortages directly affect chipmaking hub Hsinchu County. Source: Taipei Times, March 27, 2026 (https://www.taipeitimes.com/News/front/archives/2026/03/27/2003854549).
drought_chip_farm.rationale: A confirmed government water-rationing order at Taiwan's primary chip-fab hub (Hsinchu) satisfies the rubric's Level 2 criterion of 'a confirmed production curtailment / water-rationing order at a fab.' The US Southwest simultaneously shows D3/D4 drought with record-low snowpack across AZ, NM, CO, UT (NCEI/USDM, June 2026), but no fab-level curtailment is confirmed there, making Taiwan the load-bearing Level 2 trigger.

dissent.view: US Southwest drought (AZ, NM) is at D3–D4 with record-low April snowpack but no confirmed fab or farm production curtailment reported as of late June 2026; TSMC Arizona is not yet in full production. This alone would score Level 1.

reservoir_stress.fact: USBR Lower Colorado Weekly Hydrologic Update (June 15, 2026) reports Lake Mead elevation at 1,047.14 feet, equating to 29% of capacity. Circle of Blue (published May 31 / updated June 24, 2026) confirms Mead is approximately 12 feet above the 1,035-foot hydropower-capacity cliff at Hoover Dam. Sources: https://www.usbr.gov/lc/region/g4000/weekly.pdf (June 15, 2026); https://www.circleofblue.org/2026/water-energy/hoover-dam-approaches-a-hydropower-cliff/
reservoir_stress.rationale: At 1,047 ft, Lake Mead is at 29% of capacity — below the 30% CRITICAL threshold — and is only ~12 feet above the 1,035-ft Hoover Dam hydropower generation cliff, which is within the rubric's 15-foot critical-proximity criterion. Both CRITICAL conditions are simultaneously met.

dissent.view: EarthSky (May 26, 2026) cites current Lake Mead level at 1,052 feet, slightly higher than the June 15 USBR weekly reading of 1,047.14 ft — a minor discrepancy likely reflecting the rapid decline between late May and mid-June.

fao_food_trend.fact: FAO Food Price Index (FFPI) averaged 130.8 points in May 2026, down 0.2 points (0.2%) from its revised April level of ~131.0, which was the highest reading since January 2023; April 2026 was +2.0% YoY. Source: fao.org/worldfoodsituation/foodpricesindex/en, released ~5 June 2026.
fao_food_trend.rationale: MoM change is −0.2% (essentially flat, well within the <3% threshold and not a rise), while YoY remains positive (~+2%), so the index does not meet the FALLING criterion (requires down both MoM and YoY). With MoM change <3% and 3-month cumulative rise (March 128.5 → May 130.8) of only ~1.8%, the SPIKING threshold (MoM ≥5% or 3-month ≥10%) is not met, placing the indicator squarely at ELEVATED.

china_export_curbs.fact: In December 2025, the NDRC suspended phosphate fertilizer exports through August 2026. In mid-March 2026, Beijing extended restrictions to nitrogen-potassium blends and additional phosphate varieties (reported by Reuters/Bloomberg). A limited urea export quota of 1.5–1.6 million tonnes was issued on ~May 26, 2026 with a minimum price floor of $660/t FOB prilled and $670/t granular — remaining under tight quota management. Phosphate suspension and NK bans remain active. Sources: Profercy (May 26, 2026); Exiger/potatopro/agricultureofamerica (March–April 2026); SunSirs citing NDRC meeting (December 2025).
china_export_curbs.rationale: The mid-March 2026 extension of export bans to NK blends and additional phosphate varieties, plus the April 2026 sulphuric acid export ban, constitute new/broadened restrictions added within the trailing ~3 months, qualifying as a TIGHTENED (score 2) regime; the subsequent limited urea quota re-issuance carries a price floor and volume cap that confirms continued tight control, not a lifting.

dissent.view: The Philippines reportedly received assurances from China that fertilizer exports would not be restricted, though Chinese authorities never publicly confirmed this position (Reuters/Agriculture of America, March 2026).

Some industry participants argue the urea quota re-issuance in late May 2026 signals a partial easing rather than continued tightening, though the quota is volume-capped and subject to strict price floors (Profercy, May 26, 2026).

taiwan_tension.fact: As of June 26, 2026, the ISW/AEI China & Taiwan Update reports ongoing CCG incursions (4 near Kinmen, 1 near Pratas in May) and ADIZ incursions at rates 'markedly decreased from previous averages exceeding 300 per month,' with no new named large-scale PLA drill in 2026 H1. The most recent significant military event was the PLA carrier Fujian transiting the Taiwan Strait (~June 24, 2026, SCMP) amid Taiwan's Immediate Combat Readiness Exercise — characterized by PLA Eastern Theater Command as 'routine training.' The last named blockade-rehearsal drill was Justice Mission 2025 (Dec 29–30, 2025). No kinetic clash, actual blockade, or full mobilization has been reported. Source: ISW/AEI China & Taiwan Update, June 26, 2026 (https://www.aei.org/articles/china-taiwan-update-june-26-2026/); SCMP, ~June 24, 2026 (https://www.scmp.com/news/china/military/article/3358227).
taiwan_tension.rationale: Activity is assertive — persistent CCG incursions, carrier transits, and grey-zone coercion — but ADIZ incursion rates are declining from prior peaks and no new named large-scale blockade drill has occurred in 2026 H1, ruling out Level 0 (calm/routine). The absence of any kinetic clash, actual blockade, or full mobilization rules out Level 2 (crisis), placing the indicator squarely at Level 1 (elevated).

dissent.view: Some analysts (Stimson Center's Yun Sun, Taipei Times, Jan 2026) argue the structural trend — normalized drills, eroded median line, contiguous zone incursions — represents a slow-motion crisis that could tip quickly in 2026, implying the rubric's Level 1 understates cumulative risk.

fab_concentration.fact: As of Q1 2026, TSMC's only operational overseas fab (Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1) produces at 4nm/N4 — not ≤3nm. All ≤3nm capacity (3nm at ~200,000 WPM and 2nm at ~90,000–100,000 WPM) is located exclusively in Taiwan (Fab 18B Tainan and Fab 20/Fab 22 Hsinchu/Kaohsiung). Arizona Fab 21 Phase 2 (3nm-capable) has completed construction but equipment installation begins Q3 2026, with volume production targeted for 2H 2027. Japan Kumamoto Fab 2 (3nm) is slated for 2028. Taiwanese law explicitly bars TSMC from deploying its most advanced node (currently 2nm) overseas. TSMC's Q1 2026 earnings presentation confirms 3nm = 25% of revenue and 5nm = 36%, all from Taiwan fabs. Institutional estimates place overseas share of total capacity at ~20% by 2028, with leading-edge (sub-2nm) mix reaching ~30% outside Taiwan only by 2030. Sources: TSMC Q1 2026 SEC Form 6-K (sec.gov, April 2026); TrendForce (April 27, 2026); Focus Taiwan/CNA (April 16, 2026); Tom's Hardware (December 18, 2025); Taipei Times / MOEA (November 2024).
fab_concentration.rationale: Zero ≤3nm wafer capacity is operational outside Taiwan as of June 30, 2026 — the only overseas operational fab (Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1) runs at 4nm, and the next overseas ≤3nm fab (Arizona Phase 2) will not enter volume production until 2H 2027 at the earliest. This maps directly to rubric level 2: <10% outside Taiwan AND no operational overseas leading-edge fab.

dissent.view: Institutional investors cited by Commercial Times suggest TSMC's overseas capacity could account for ~20% of total capacity by 2028, and sub-2nm nodes could reach 30% outside Taiwan by 2030 — implying an accelerating trajectory toward rubric level 1, but these are forward projections, not current operational figures.

Some sources (abhs.in, March 2026) describe TSMC Arizona as producing 'leading-edge' chips, potentially classifying N4 as leading-edge; under a looser definition that includes N4/N5, Arizona Fab 21 Phase 1 would represent a small but non-zero overseas leading-edge share.

treasury_clearing_rollout.fact: TBAC Q2 2026 presentation (May 2026, home.treasury.gov/system/files/221/TBACCharge1Q22026.pdf) states: 'Largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation associated with the bilateral agreements between participants' and 'it is possible that clearing will not be available to all participants in all regions by the deadline.' As of April 17, 2026 (SEC.gov), two requests for exemptive relief—SIFMA's inter-affiliate expansion and IIB's extraterritorial carve-out—were published for comment with a May 29, 2026 deadline, meaning both remain unresolved six months before the cash go-live date of Dec 31, 2026.
treasury_clearing_rollout.rationale: Pending exemptive relief on inter-affiliate and extraterritorial scope (SIFMA and IIB petitions still open as of May 2026), combined with TBAC's explicit identification of a documentation backlog in bilateral agreements, maps directly to the rubric's Friction criteria ('documentation backlog, pending exemptive relief'). No deadline extension has been announced and the SEC reaffirmed the Dec 31, 2026 / Jun 30, 2027 deadlines in March 2026, precluding a score of 2; but the unresolved scope issues and bespoke legal negotiation burden preclude a clean score of 0.

dissent.view: SEC Chair Atkins (March 10, 2026, Bloomberg) and Commissioner Uyeda (April 20, 2026, SEC.gov) publicly reaffirmed commitment to the Dec 2026 deadline with no indication of further extension, suggesting regulators view the process as on-track despite friction.

OFR data cited in TBAC May 2026 shows ~58% of in-scope repo volumes already centrally cleared in the first 8 months of 2025, and cash market is described as 'relatively better positioned vs repo,' suggesting the market is making material progress ahead of deadlines.
